mnemonics.title: System Call Categories
mnemonics.content: P-FID-P: PROCESS CONTROL, FILE MANAGEMENT, INFORMATION MAINTENANCE, DEVICE MANAGEMENT, PROTECTION, COMMUNICATION - REMEMBER AS 'PEOPLE FIND IT DANGEROUS, PROTECT YOURSELF' (P-FID-P: Process File Information Device Protection)

mnemonics.title: Process Control Functions
mnemonics.content: FORK EXEC WAIT EXIT - REMEMBER 'FOR EXCELLENT WORK, EXIT' (FORK to create, EXEC new program, WAIT for child, EXIT gracefully)

mnemonics.title: File Management Operations
mnemonics.content: CREATE OPEN READ WRITE CLOSE DELETE - REMEMBER 'C OR W CD' (Create, Open, Read, Write, Close, Delete)

acronyms.expansion: Fork Outer Resource Transfer - creates child with copied address space
acronyms.usage: Used when you need to create a new process in Unix/Linux systems

acronyms.expansion: Execute External Code - replaces current process image with new program
acronyms.usage: Used when you want to run a different program in the current process
Used for device configuration and special operations not covered by standard calls

acronyms.expansion: Process Identifier - unique number identifying each process
acronyms.usage: Used by parent to track child processes, obtained via getpid()

acronyms.expansion: Inter-Process Communication - mechanisms for processes to communicate
acronyms.usage: Includes pipes, message queues, shared memory, sockets

visualTips.visualization: Think of fork() as making a perfect photocopy of yourself. Both the original (parent) and the photocopy (child) continue executing from the same point, but now there are two of you with identical instructions.

Imagine each process has a desk with numbered slots (file descriptors). Slot 0 is your inbox (stdin), slot 1 is your outbox (stdout), slot 2 is your error box (stderr). Opening files adds more numbered slots.

Think of system calls as a security checkpoint between user space (restaurants) and kernel space (kitchen). You (user process) cannot directly access kitchen equipment; you must request dishes through the counter (system call interface).

analogies.analogy: System calls are like ordering food from a restaurant menu - you make a request, the kitchen (OS kernel) prepares it with special equipment, and you get the result. Regular function calls are like cooking in your own kitchen - you have direct access to everything.
fork() is like cell division in biology. One cell (parent process) splits into two identical cells (parent and child), each continuing life with the same genetic material (program code).

File descriptors are like table numbers in a restaurant. When you open a file (get a table), you receive a number (file descriptor) to reference your table in future interactions (read/write operations).
fork() is like getting a blank canvas, and exec() is like painting a completely new picture on that canvas. First you create the canvas (fork), then you replace the content with something new (exec).
